text: The Funding Packages section outlines how graduate students at Queen’s University are financially supported throughout their degree programs. Across funding sources, graduate students receive nearly $28 million annually. Students are encouraged to understand how funding packages function and to consult the Graduate Funding Guide for detailed information on financial resources and supports.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: For the 2025–26 academic year, full-time PhD students within funding-eligible years (typically years 1–4) receive a guaranteed minimum funding package of $23,000 per year. Many departments establish higher minimum guarantees and offer higher average funding amounts.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Most full-time Master’s students in non-professional programs receive funding support. One-year programs are typically funded in year one, while two-year programs are typically funded in years one and two. Students should consult their program and supervisor for specific funding details.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: A funding package details a student’s committed funding for each academic year and is normally communicated in July or August preceding the funded year. Funding packages may include internal awards or fellowships, external awards, teaching assistantships (TAs), teaching fellowships (TFs), research assistantships (RAs), and Graduate Research Fellowships (GRFs).

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Internal awards and fellowships are Queen’s-based funds that may be merit-based or donor-funded. External awards, such as Tri-Council funding or the Ontario Graduate Scholarship, are competitive and count toward the university’s minimum funding guarantee rather than being additional to it.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Teaching Assistantships (TAs), Teaching Fellowships (TFs), and Research Assistantships (RAs) are employment-based funding opportunities that provide teaching or research experience. Graduate Research Fellowships (GRFs) are typically supervisor-funded and support research directly related to a student’s own thesis or program requirements. Declining a TA, TF, or RA included in a funding package results in a reduction of the committed funding amount.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Doctoral students must be registered full-time in years 1–4, maintain good academic standing, be eligible for departmental funding sources, apply for major scholarships for which they are eligible, and have indicated financial need at the time of admission in order to qualify for the minimum funding guarantee.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Tuition and ancillary fees are not covered by the university as part of a funding package. Students are responsible for paying these costs from their funding, savings, employment income, or other financial resources.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Eligible graduate students may apply for the Ontario Student Assistance Program (OSAP), which includes repayable loans and non-repayable grants based on financial need. Students may choose to accept only the grant portion. International students are encouraged to explore equivalent funding programs available in their home countries.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Funding disbursement depends on the source. Internal and external awards are typically paid at the beginning of each academic term. TA and RA funding is distributed over the duration of the contract, usually monthly or bi-weekly. Students must provide banking information for direct deposit.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: If a funded student changes from full-time to part-time or inactive status, withdraws, or takes a leave of absence, their funding may be reduced, suspended, or terminated. Funding is not guaranteed for part-time or inactive students, and future funding packages may be affected.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Students on an approved leave of absence are not eligible to receive internal awards during the leave. Awards are suspended and reinstated upon return, and funding eligibility periods are extended by the length of the leave. Eligible students may access up to $5,000 one time during a general leave, and doctoral students may access up to $10,000 total during maternity or parental leave.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Students who exceed their funding-eligible period may seek additional support through TA, RA, or TF employment, GRFs, or internal and external awards at the discretion of their supervisor and graduate program. Some students may transition to part-time status while engaging in full-time employment.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Students experiencing financial difficulty should first consult their supervisor or advisor. If concerns remain unresolved, they may escalate to the Graduate Coordinator, Faculty Associate Dean (Graduate Studies), or the Associate Dean at the School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs. Emergency bursaries and financial assistance programs are available through multiple campus units.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs

text: Graduate students are strongly encouraged to secure housing in Kingston before beginning their studies. For information about the Kingston housing market, students should consult the Off-Campus Housing Resource Guide for Graduate Students.

metadata.unit: School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs
